section.title: Paul's Ministry and Travel Plans
section.sectionRationale: Having reached the summit of his argument, Paul turns personal. He explains why he wrote so boldly to a church he did not found: because of the grace God gave him to be a minister of Christ to the Gentiles, a priestly service in which the nations become an offering acceptable to God, sanctified by the Spirit. He describes his ambition to preach where Christ was not named, lays out his plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain after delivering the collection to the poor saints in Jerusalem, and asks earnestly for prayer, closing with the blessing of the God of peace.
section.thesis: Paul's whole apostolic labor is a priestly ministry of the gospel by which the Gentiles are offered up to God, sanctified by the Spirit; and this God-centered mission shapes his ambition, his travel plans, and his humble request for the prayers of the church, all resting in the God of peace.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul's bold reminder and his priestly ministry to the Gentiles; his ambition to preach where Christ was not named; his travel plans by way of Rome to Spain after the collection for Jerusalem; and his request for prayer and the blessing of the God of peace.

units.label: A priestly ministry to the Gentiles
units.note: the Gentiles an offering acceptable to God

verses.text: I myself am convinced, my brothers, that you yourselves are full of goodness, brimming with knowledge, and able to instruct one another.
However, I have written you a bold reminder on some points, because of the grace God has given me

to be a {{leitourgos|minister}} of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles in the {{hierourgeo|priestly service}} of the gospel of God, so that the Gentiles might become an {{prosphora|offering}} acceptable to God, sanctified by the Holy Spirit.
Therefore I exult in Christ Jesus in my service to God.

units.label: Ambition to preach where Christ is not named

verses.text: I will not presume to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through me in leading the Gentiles to obedience by word and deed,
by the power of signs and wonders, and by the power of the Spirit of God. So from Jerusalem all the way around to Illyricum, I have fully proclaimed the gospel of Christ.
In this way I have aspired to preach the gospel where Christ was not known, so that I would not be building on someone else’s {{themelios|foundation}}.
Rather, as it is written: “Those who were not told about Him will see, and those who have not heard will understand.”
That is why I have often been hindered from coming to you.

units.label: Travel plans by way of Rome to Spain
units.note: the collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem

verses.text: But now that there are no further opportunities for me in these regions, and since I have longed for many years to visit you,
I hope to see you on my way to Spain. And after I have enjoyed your company for a while, you can equip me for my journey.
Now, however, I am on my way to Jerusalem to serve the saints there.
For Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a contribution for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem.
They were pleased to do it, and indeed they owe it to them. For if the Gentiles have shared in their spiritual blessings, they are obligated to minister to them with material blessings.
So after I have completed this service and have safely delivered this bounty to them, I will set off to Spain by way of you.
I know that when I come to you, I will come in the fullness of the blessing of Christ.

units.label: A request for prayer and the God of peace
units.note: join me in my struggle by praying to God

verses.text: Now I urge you, brothers, by our Lord Jesus Christ and by the love of the Spirit, to join me in my struggle by praying to God for me.
Pray that I may be delivered from the unbelievers in Judea, and that my service in Jerusalem may be acceptable to the saints there,
so that by God’s will I may come to you with joy and together with you be refreshed.
The God of {{eirene|peace}} be with all of you. Amen.

leitourgos.gloss: A minister or public servant, often with a sacred, priestly sense; the related words describe temple service and worship. Paul casts himself as a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles, serving at the altar of the gospel. His apostolic labor is not merely administrative but priestly, offering up the nations to God.

hierourgeo.gloss: To perform sacred service, to work as a priest, to minister in holy things. Paul uses this striking priestly verb of his preaching of the gospel: proclaiming Christ is his altar work. The temple imagery reframes evangelism as worship, in which the herald serves God by bringing the nations near.

prosphora.gloss: An offering, that which is presented or brought to God, especially a sacrifice. The converted Gentiles are themselves the offering Paul brings to the altar, made acceptable to God and set apart by the Holy Spirit. The fruit of the gospel is a living sacrifice of redeemed people presented to God for His glory.

themelios.gloss: A foundation, the base on which a building rests. Paul aspired to preach where Christ was not yet named, so as not to build on another's foundation but to lay the gospel's groundwork in unreached places. The image fits his pioneering, frontier ambition to carry the good news to the ends of the earth.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being; the Hebrew shalom stands behind it. Paul closes with the God of peace, the God who reconciles enemies to Himself and to one another through Christ. In a letter that heals division between strong and weak, Jew and Gentile, the blessing of the God of peace is the fitting seal.

basic.context: Paul now explains himself. Why write so boldly to a church he had never visited? Because God gave him grace to be the apostle to the Gentiles. He describes his ministry in priestly terms: preaching the gospel is his altar service, and the converted nations are the offering he brings to God. He tells of his ambition to reach unreached places, his plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain after taking a relief gift to Jerusalem, and his heartfelt request for prayer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the personal, missionary close of the letter's body. Read it to see how doctrine drives mission and prayer. The priestly language of verse 16 is deliberate: evangelism is worship, and people are the sacrifice. The travel plans are not filler; they show the collection uniting Gentile and Jewish believers and reveal Paul's dependence on God and on the prayers of the church.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls himself a leitourgos, a priestly minister of Christ, engaged in hierourgeo, the sacred service of the gospel, so that the Gentiles become a prosphora, an offering acceptable to God. He aspired not to build on another's themelios, foundation, but to reach the unreached. And he ends with the God of eirene, peace, be with you all.
basic.typology: The temple, the altar, and the sacrifices find a new fulfillment: the gospel is the priestly service, and redeemed people are the offering. The relief gift for Jerusalem echoes the promise that the wealth of the nations would flow to Zion. Paul's mission to the ends of the earth carries forward Israel's calling to be a light to the nations.
basic.christ: Paul does not presume to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through him. Christ is the content of his preaching, the power behind his mission, and the one in whom he exults. He comes to Rome in the fullness of the blessing of Christ. The whole enterprise is Christ's work through His servant, leading the nations to the obedience of faith.
basic.god: The ministry is the priestly service of the gospel of God, and its aim is an offering acceptable to God, sanctified by the Spirit. Paul serves God, exults before God, plans by the will of God, and begs prayer to God. The passage is saturated with dependence on God, closing with the God of peace, whose reconciling character crowns the letter.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 52 stands behind Paul's ambition to reach those who had not heard. Romans 12 called believers to present themselves as a living sacrifice, which the priestly imagery here echoes. First Corinthians 16 and Second Corinthians 8 and 9 describe the same Jerusalem collection. Philippians 4 speaks of the God of peace.
basic.oneStory: God had promised that the nations would come to His light and that their gifts would flow to Zion. Here Paul, priest of the gospel, is gathering that harvest: the Gentiles offered up to God, a relief gift binding the nations to Jerusalem, and the good news pressing on to the ends of the earth. The one story of God moves outward through Paul toward Spain and beyond, carried by the God of peace.

context.literary: This is the epilogue to the letter's body, verses 14 to 33, before the greetings of chapter sixteen. It mirrors the letter's opening: Paul again speaks of the grace given him, his apostleship to the Gentiles, and the obedience of faith. The tone shifts from argument to autobiography, mission report, itinerary, and prayer, ending with a benediction that anticipates the closing greetings.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57, likely from Corinth at the close of his third missionary journey, about to carry the collection from the Gentile churches of Macedonia and Achaia to the poor believers in Jerusalem. He hoped, after delivering it, to reach Rome and press on to Spain, the western frontier. His fears about Judea proved well founded, as Acts records his arrest in Jerusalem.
context.canonical: The passage is a key witness to the geography and strategy of early Christian mission and to the Jerusalem collection, a project Paul treats as both practical relief and a sign of the unity of Jew and Gentile in Christ. Its priestly view of evangelism has profoundly shaped Christian understanding of mission as worship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain why Paul wrote so boldly to Rome, to describe his God-given priestly ministry to the Gentiles and his pioneering mission strategy, to prepare the Roman church for his visit on the way to Spain, and to enlist their earnest prayers for his dangerous journey to Jerusalem.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The travel plans and personal circumstances describe Paul's situation. The abiding truths are prescriptive: gospel ministry is priestly service offered to God, mission rightly presses toward the unreached, believers owe material care across ethnic lines, and the church must strive together in prayer, resting in the God of peace.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by the priestly service of the gospel?

views.label: Evangelism as worship, the Gentiles as the offering
views.case: The historic reading takes the temple language seriously: preaching the gospel is Paul's altar work, and the converted nations, sanctified by the Spirit, are the sacrifice he presents to God, in continuity with the living sacrifice of Romans 12.

views.label: A merely figurative flourish
views.case: Some treat the priestly words as decorative metaphor with little weight; but the density of the imagery, minister, priestly service, offering, sanctified, shows Paul means to reframe mission as genuine worship.

debates.bearing: The historic reading dignifies gospel ministry as worship offered to God and honors the converted as a holy offering, guarding against both a bloodless view of mission and any notion of a repeated atoning sacrifice.

debates.question: Why does Paul insist on preaching where Christ is not named?

views.label: A pioneering call to the unreached
views.case: The historic reading sees a Spirit-given ambition to lay the gospel foundation in places that have never heard, fulfilling Isaiah 52:15, rather than building on another's work; this drives his plan to reach Spain.

views.label: A rule forbidding all ministry in established churches
views.case: Some over-read it as a universal principle against ever serving where others have labored; but Paul plainly plans to minister among the Roman believers and be equipped by them, so it is his particular calling, not a law for all.

debates.bearing: The historic reading honors the missionary drive to reach the unreached while leaving room for building up existing churches, as Paul intends to do at Rome on his way west.

study.originalLanguages: Paul stacks up temple words to describe his life's work. He is a leitourgos, a priestly minister of Christ to the Gentiles, engaged in hierourgeo, sacred altar service, as he preaches the gospel of God; and the redeemed nations are the prosphora, the offering he brings, made acceptable and set apart by the Holy Spirit. His ambition is to reach where Christ is unnamed, so as not to build on another's themelios, foundation, but to break fresh ground. And after all his plans, he rests the whole church in the God of eirene, peace, the reconciling God whose shalom fittingly seals a letter that heals division.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as Paul's gospel preaching becomes priestly service and the converted Gentiles become the acceptable offering, sanctified by the Spirit, echoing Romans 12:1.

typology.type: The wealth of the nations flowing to Zion
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadowed in the collection the Gentile churches send to the poor saints in Jerusalem, binding the nations to God's ancient people.
Israel's calling to be a light reaches out through Paul to those who had not heard, fulfilling Isaiah 52:15 as the gospel presses to the ends of the earth.

study.christ: Christ is the sum of Paul's ministry. He will not speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through him, leading the Gentiles to the obedience of faith by word and deed and by the Spirit's power. Paul exults in Christ Jesus in his service to God, and he expects to come to Rome in the fullness of the blessing of Christ. The mission to the ends of the earth is Christ's own work through His minister, and every convert is presented to God through the gospel of His Son.
study.god: The passage overflows with God-centeredness. Paul's ministry is grace God gave him, a priestly service of the gospel of God, aiming at an offering acceptable to God and sanctified by the Spirit. He plans by the will of God, exults in his service to God, and urges the church to pray to God for him. It closes with the God of peace, whose reconciling character is the fitting seal on a letter that has proclaimed peace with God through Christ and peace among His people.

crossReferences.note: Those who were not told will see and understand, the text driving Paul's ambition to reach the unreached.
Present your bodies as a living sacrifice; the priestly imagery here presents the converted Gentiles as an offering to God.
The generous collection from Macedonia for the poor saints, the relief gift Paul carries to Jerusalem.
Paul's arrest in Jerusalem shows how real were the dangers he asked the Romans to pray about.

study.oneStory: God long promised that the nations would stream to His light and their gifts flow to Zion. Here that promise is unfolding: Paul, priest of the gospel, offers up the redeemed Gentiles to God, carries a relief gift that binds the nations to Jerusalem, and presses the good news toward Spain and the ends of the earth. The one story that began with a promise to Abraham now spreads outward through the apostle, carried and kept by the God of peace, until the whole earth is filled with the praise of Christ.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe his ministry in verse 16?

options.text: A priestly service of the gospel, presenting the Gentiles as an offering acceptable to God, sanctified by the Spirit.
options.note: Paul frames evangelism in temple terms, with the converted as the offering.

options.text: A purely administrative task of organizing churches.
options.note: Paul did plant and order churches, but he casts his ministry in priestly terms, presenting the Gentiles as an offering to God.

options.text: A philosophical mission to debate the wise.
options.note: His calling is priestly gospel service, not primarily philosophical debate.

options.text: A commission to offer atoning sacrifices for sin.
options.note: The offering is the redeemed nations, not a repeated sacrifice for sin.

quiz.q: What was Paul's ambition regarding where he preached?

options.text: To preach where Christ was not known, so as not to build on another's foundation.
options.note: Paul aspired to pioneer among the unreached, fulfilling Isaiah 52:15.

options.text: To minister only in well-established churches.
options.note: His ambition was the opposite, to reach places that had never heard.

options.text: To avoid all travel and settle in one city.
options.note: Paul planned extensive travel toward Spain and beyond.

options.text: To confine his work to Jewish synagogues alone.
options.note: Paul did go to the Jew first, but his ambition was frontier ground where Christ had not yet been named, among the Gentiles.

quiz.q: What was Paul planning to do before visiting Rome and Spain?

options.text: Deliver the collection from the Gentile churches to the poor saints in Jerusalem.
options.note: Paul was on his way to serve the Jerusalem believers with the relief gift.

options.text: Return to his hometown to rest.
options.note: He was heading to Jerusalem with the collection, not home to rest.

options.text: Plant a church in Illyricum first.
options.note: Paul had already preached as far as Illyricum, but his immediate plan was to carry the collection to Jerusalem before Rome and Spain.

options.text: Attend a council of the apostles.
options.note: His stated purpose was to bring the collection to the poor saints.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say the Gentiles owe material help to the Jerusalem believers?

options.text: Because having shared in their spiritual blessings, they are obligated to minister with material blessings.
options.note: Spiritual debt to Israel calls for material generosity in return.

options.text: Because Jerusalem demanded a tax from them.
options.note: It was a willing contribution, not a demanded tax.

options.text: Only because Paul personally pressured them.
options.note: The churches gave gladly and freely; the true ground Paul names is that sharing in Jewish spiritual blessings creates a joyful obligation to give materially.

options.text: Because the law of Moses required it of Gentiles.
options.note: The ground is shared spiritual blessing, not a legal requirement.

quiz.q: What did Paul ask the Roman church to pray for?

options.text: That he be delivered in Judea and that his service in Jerusalem be acceptable, so he could come to them with joy.
options.note: Paul asks them to strive with him in prayer for deliverance and a fruitful visit.

options.text: That he would gain fame throughout the empire.
options.note: He asks for deliverance and acceptable service, not personal fame.

options.text: That he would be spared all further travel.
options.note: Paul did seek deliverance in Judea, but not for the sake of ease; he longed to travel on to Rome with joy.

options.text: That the collection would make him wealthy.
options.note: The collection was for the poor saints; his prayer was for deliverance and fruitful service.

questions: Paul saw preaching the gospel as priestly service, presenting people to God as an offering. How would seeing evangelism as an act of worship reshape the way you share Christ with others?
Paul refused to speak of anything except what Christ accomplished through him. How can you keep Christ, rather than yourself, at the center of the good you do?
Paul burned to reach those who had never heard of Christ. What place do the unreached have in your prayers, giving, and imagination?
The Gentile churches gave materially to believers they had never met, out of shared spiritual blessing. Who has blessed you spiritually, and how might you respond with generosity across differences?
Paul urged the church to strive together with him in prayer for his dangerous journey. Whose gospel labors is God calling you to support in earnest, wrestling prayer this week?

translationNotes.note: Minister renders leitourgos and priestly service renders a verb for sacred, priestly work; the offering is the converted Gentiles, presented to God and sanctified by the Spirit, not a repeated atoning sacrifice.
The quotation is from Isaiah 52:15, following the Greek Old Testament, and grounds Paul's ambition to preach where Christ has not yet been named so that those who never heard may understand.
The fullness of the blessing of Christ; some later manuscripts add of the gospel, but the sense is the same, that Paul expects to come bearing Christ's rich blessing to the Roman church.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 15:14-33. Poetic quotation lines in verse 21 were concatenated into the running verse text with single spaces. No em dashes required substitution. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
